items.q: Why do brighter-looking stars not necessarily have higher luminosity?
items.correct: Apparent brightness depends on both luminosity and distance — a closer dim star can look brighter than a distant luminous one.
items.misconception: Brighter-looking stars are always intrinsically more luminous.
items.random_wrong: Apparent brightness depends only on the star's temperature.
items.why: Students don't naturally factor in distance; they assume 'looks bright' means 'is bright', missing the inverse-square law applied to flux.

items.q: What does the cosmological redshift of distant galaxies indicate?
items.correct: Space itself is expanding, stretching the wavelength of light from distant galaxies as it travels.
items.misconception: Galaxies are moving through space away from us, like objects flying outward from a centre.
items.random_wrong: The galaxies are turning red because they are getting older.
items.why: Students apply the Doppler effect literally; the expansion of space is a more subtle picture they often miss.

items.q: What are typical units for the Hubble constant?
items.correct: km per second per megaparsec (km/s/Mpc) — recession speed per unit distance.
items.misconception: Metres per second — it's the speed at which the universe expands.
items.random_wrong: Light-years — it's a measure of cosmic distance.
items.why: Students treat H0 as a speed or distance rather than a rate of expansion (speed per unit distance), missing the structure of Hubble's law.

items.q: A star with m = 1 compared to m = 6 is:
items.why: It's 100× brighter. “10× brighter” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Absolute magnitude is defined at a distance of:
items.why: It's 10 pc. “1 pc” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
